title: Galois Action on Higher etale Homotopy Groups
speaker: Alexander Petrov
abstract: Given an algebraic variety over a number field F, one can attach to it its etale cohomology groups, etale fundamental group, and higher etale homotopy groups, all equipped with an action of the absolute Galois group of F. The Galois action on etale cohomology is known to satisfy several special properties: for example, it is de Rham at places above p (where we consider etale cohomology with Q_p-coefficients) and eigenvalues of Frobenius elements are Weil numbers. Analogous facts, appropriately formulated, also hold for the Galois action on the fundamental group. On the contrary, Galois action on higher etale homotopy groups turns out to fail some of these properties. In this talk, I will discuss the observation that (dual of) higher etale homotopy groups of varieties over numbers fields often contain subrepresentations that are not de Rham at p. In our examples this stems from the difference between the cohomology of an arithmetic group and its pro-finite completion, and I will also discuss this (well-known) phenomenon, which turns out to behave very differently depending on whether the ambient reductive group of the arithmetic group is of Hodge type. This talk is based on joint works with Lue Pan and George Pappas.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Geometric Galois representations from cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by recalling that étale cohomology of a variety over a number field carries Galois representations satisfying geometric conditions: unramifiedness almost everywhere, de Rhamness at p-adic places, and Weil-number Frobenius eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian fundamental groups and pro-algebraic completion
proof_stages.summary: The speaker passes from cohomology to the nonabelian fundamental group, explains topological and étale π_1 and its pro-algebraic completion, and states that finite-dimensional Galois subrepresentations in its coordinate ring still satisfy the same geometric conditions as cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Étale homotopy type and the simply connected comparison
proof_stages.summary: The étale homotopy type is presented as a profinite completion of the topological homotopy type via finite spaces, with unchanged finite-coefficient cohomology, and in the simply connected case higher étale homotopy groups are just profinite completions of the usual homotopy groups and remain controlled by rational homotopy theory.

proof_stages.label: Failure of homotopy-groupwise completion for A_g
proof_stages.summary: Using the moduli stack A_g of principally polarized abelian varieties, which is topologically a K(π,1), the speaker shows that the analogous étale K(π,1) statement fails: congruence subgroup properties and Galois-action constraints force the natural cohomology map from the profinite fundamental group to étale cohomology to vanish, so higher étale homotopy must be present.

proof_stages.label: Non-de Rham extension in π₂ of A_g
proof_stages.summary: The main proposition asserts that for g at least two the dual of the second étale homotopy group of A_g contains a Galois-stable nonsplit extension of Q_p by Q_p(-1), hence gives a higher-homotopical Galois representation that is not de Rham and so is unlike representations coming from cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Derived cohomology mechanism via Hodge-bundle classes
proof_stages.summary: The source of the nonsplit extensions is a Galois-equivariant map of cochain complexes from continuous cohomology of Sp_{2g}(Z_p) to cochains on A_g that is zero on ordinary cohomology but nonzero in the derived category, with generators l_{4i-1} detected by products of Hodge-bundle Chern-character classes and Galois cohomology classes κ_i.

proof_stages.label: Contrast with GL_n and p-adic regulators
proof_stages.summary: The final comparison with GL_n arithmetic groups emphasizes that, without an algebraic-geometric Galois action of Hodge type, the analogous stable p-adic-to-discrete cohomology map is expected to be nonzero or surjective by p-adic regulator conjectures, highlighting the specificity of the A_g phenomenon.

examples.name: Nodal curve from gluing two rational points
examples.note: A smooth proper curve with two rational points identified illustrates that even cohomological extensions of mixed Hodge structures or Galois representations can remember geometric data such as the difference of the two points in the Jacobian.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the nodal curve by gluing two rational points and analyzes its first cohomology exact sequence and extension class depending on the point difference

passes.reason: A nodal curve made by identifying two points is a singular boundary degeneration, used only to illustrate extension classes in H^1.
gluing two points produces a singular collapsed nodal degeneration, used only to illustrate nontrivial extension classes.
Gluing two points gives a singular nodal boundary degeneration of a smooth curve, used to illustrate nontrivial extension data rather than refute a claim.
It is a singular boundary degeneration of a smooth curve obtained by identifying two points, used only to illustrate extension classes.
A nodal curve obtained by identifying two points is a collapsed boundary degeneration used only to illustrate extension classes.
A nodal curve obtained by gluing two points is a collapsed boundary degeneration, used only to illustrate extension classes.
A nodal curve is a singular boundary degeneration obtained by identifying two points, used to illustrate nontrivial cohomological/Galois extensions.
It is a singular quotient/boundary degeneration of a smooth curve, used only to illustrate mixed or Galois extension classes.
A nodal curve obtained by identifying two points is a singular/collapsed degeneration, used to illustrate nontrivial extensions rather than to refute a claim.
A singular nodal degeneration of a smooth curve, used to illustrate how H^1 extensions remember the glued points.
a singular curve obtained by identifying two points, used to illustrate mixed/Galois extension data rather than to refute a claim
a one-node singular degeneration of a smooth curve, used to illustrate extension data in cohomology

examples.name: Elliptic curve
examples.note: In response to a question, the elliptic curve case is used to show that for an abelian fundamental group the pro-algebraic completion produces no essentially new Galois representations beyond those coming from first homology.
examples.engagement_reason: when asked, the speaker specializes the pro-algebraic/Galois representation discussion to an elliptic curve, using its abelian fundamental group to conclude no new representations beyond homology

passes.reason: An elliptic curve is used as a standard completely understood abelian-fundamental-group case, not to refute a claim.
a generic elliptic curve is a standard genus-one curve, used as a simple case where no new representations appear.

examples.name: The thrice-punctured projective line
examples.note: The open curve P^1 minus three points is mentioned as an orientation example whose large nonabelian fundamental group and Belyi-theoretic Galois action contrast with the elliptic curve case and later with congruence-subgroup phenomena.
examples.engagement_reason: the thrice-punctured projective line is invoked as a known large/faithful Galois-action example via Belyi, without carrying out a computation or construction on it

passes.reason: The thrice-punctured projective line is the minimal rigid hyperbolic punctured sphere and is also invoked as witnessing failure of a congruence-subgroup-property expectation.

The thrice-punctured line is the minimal hyperbolic punctured sphere with high puncture symmetry, and is invoked via Belyi as witnessing failure of a congruence-subgroup-type expectation.

examples.name: Stable cohomology of GL_n(Z) and GL_n(Z_p)
examples.note: The GL_n comparison illustrates a different regulator-driven arithmetic-group situation where the p-adic-to-discrete cohomology map is expected to be nonzero or surjective, unlike the Hodge-type vanishing phenomenon for A_g.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sets up the stable cohomology map for GL_n(Z_p) to GL_n(Z), identifies the stable generators and degrees, and discusses the regulator/nonvanishing behavior

passes.reason: The stable GL_n cohomology is a rank-limiting object used as a contrasting nonvanishing regulator case obstructing an algebraic-geometric homotopy interpretation.
the stable n→∞ cohomology is a limiting object and is used as a contrast/obstruction to extending the geometric vanishing phenomenon to GL_n arithmetic groups.
Stable cohomology is an n-to-infinity limiting invariant, and the nonzero p-adic regulator comparison is used as a non-geometric obstruction/contrast to the vanishing story.
Stable cohomology is an n-to-infinity limiting object and is used as a contrasting obstruction to extending the geometric vanishing/Galois-action story to GL_n.
The stable n-to-infinity cohomology of GL_n is a limiting object used as an enemy case to the algebraic-geometric vanishing/Galois-constraint phenomenon.
